title: .env Files & Secrets
summary: A .env file stores settings and secrets outside your code.
It keeps them out of the files you share.
You must never commit it to version control.

sections.text: You learned that settings and secrets belong outside your code, in environment variables. The usual place to keep them during development is a special file named .env. Using it correctly is important, because a slip can leak a secret to the world.

sections.title: What a .env File Is
sections.text: A .env file is a simple text file that holds your environment variables, the settings and secret values your app needs. Your code reads from it while you develop. Keeping these values in one separate file, instead of scattered in your code, makes them easy to manage and change.

sections.title: Never Commit It
sections.text: The single most important rule is to never commit your .env file to version control. It often holds secrets, and committing it could expose them publicly. You add the file to your ignore list so it stays on your machine only. Teams usually share a safe example file that lists the needed names, but never the real values.

media.alt: A .env file kept local and added to the ignore list, never committed

sections.title: A Diary You Keep at Home
sections.text: A .env file is like a private diary. It is useful and you refer to it often, but you keep it at home, never packed into a bag you share. Committing it would be like photocopying your diary and pinning it to a public board. The section names can be shared, but never the private contents.

media.alt: A private diary kept at home, never shared in public

sections.title: Using a .env File
items: Put your settings and secrets in a .env file.
Your code reads the values from it while developing.
Add the file to your ignore list right away.
Never commit it to version control.
Share a safe example file with only the names.

sections.text: Public code sites regularly scan for committed secrets, and an accidentally shared key can be found and misused within minutes. That is why ignoring the .env file is not optional, it is essential.

terms.definition: A file holding settings and secrets outside code.
A sensitive value like a key or password.
The shared history a .env file must stay out of.
Telling version control to skip the file.
A safe file listing the names but not the values.

questions.explanation: You must never commit a .env file to version control, since it holds secrets.
